original_abstract:
This paper introduces Deep Speech, an end-to-end speech recognition system built using a state-of-the-art deep learning architecture. In contrast to traditional speech recognition pipelines that require complex, hand-engineered components such as phoneme dictionaries and specialized features for noise robustness, our system learns directly from raw data using a simplified reconsidered recurrent neural network (RNN). By leveraging a highly optimized training framework distributed across multiple GPUs and employing novel data synthesis techniques, we are able to train on massive datasets that include thousands of hours of varied, noisy speech. 

Our approach eliminates the need for manual speaker adaptation or noise modeling, as the network learns to be inherently robust to background noise, reverberation, and speaker variation. Deep Speech achieves a 16.0% error rate on the full Switchboard Hub5’00 test set, surpassing previously published results. Furthermore, our system significantly outperforms leading commercial speech recognition platforms in challenging, real-world noisy environments, demonstrating the power and simplicity of scaling up end-to-end deep learning for speech tasks.
humanized_abstract:
In this work, we develop an end-to-end speech recognition system called Deep Speech that is based on a recent deep learning architecture. While speech recognition systems are often made up of a number of manually designed components, including phoneme lexicons and hand-engineered features designed to combat noisy audio, we show that our speech recognition system that uses a deep simplified reconsidered recurrent neural network can learn to recognize speech directly from the audio alone with little need for human intervention. To train our speech recognition system, we use a very fast and flexible training framework that we run on many GPUs and employ data synthesis techniques to train our model on hundreds of thousands of hours of speech training data, including lots of noisy audio.

We propose a deep learning based model that trains a single deep neural network to be robust to speech degradation and speaker variations without requiring speaker adaptation or explicit noise modeling. Our Deep Speech model achieves an error rate of 16.0% on the full Switchboard Hub5’00 test set, which is a new state-of-the-art performance. Interestingly, our Deep Speech model also outperforms all commercial speech recognition engines on several hard noises tests indicating that our Deep Speech model is very effective in handling real-world speech recognition challenges. One major advantage of our approach is that it is very simple to scale up the speech recognition performance. The recent breakthrough on speech recognition competition also showed that larger models can lead to better recognition performance.

While speech recognition systems are often made up of a number of manually designed components, including phoneme lexicons and hand-engineered features designed to combat noisy audio, we show that our speech recognition system that uses a deep simplified reconsidered recurrent neural network can learn to recognize speech directly from the audio alone with little need for human intervention. We propose a deep learning based model that trains a single deep neural network to be robust to speech degradation and speaker variations without requiring speaker adaptation or explicit noise modeling. One major advantage of our approach is that it is very simple to scale up the speech recognition performance. document.input:
This paper introduces Deep Speech, an end-to-end speech recognition system built using a state-of-the-art deep learning architecture. In contrast to traditional speech recognition pipelines that require complex, hand-engineered components such as phoneme dictionaries and specialized features for noise robustness, our system learns directly from raw data using a simplified reconsidered recurrent neural network (RNN). By leveraging a highly optimized training framework distributed across multiple GPUs and employing novel data synthesis techniques, we are able to train on massive datasets that include thousands of hours of varied, noisy speech. 

Our approach eliminates the need for manual speaker adaptation or noise modeling, as the network learns to be inherently robust to background noise, reverberation, and speaker variation. Deep Speech achieves a 16.0% error rate on the full Switchboard Hub5’00 test set, surpassing previously published results. Furthermore, our system significantly outperforms leading commercial speech recognition platforms in challenging, real-world noisy environments, demonstrating the power and simplicity of scaling up end-to-end deep learning for speech tasks.
